mission.title: Install and Configure Kubescape on Kubernetes
mission.description: Kubescape is an open-source Kubernetes security platform that provides comprehensive security coverage across the entire development and deployment lifecycle. You would install it to enhance the security posture of your Kubernetes clusters through risk analysis, compliance checks, and misconfiguration scanning.

steps.title: Add the Helm repository
steps.description:
Add the Kubescape Helm repository to your local Helm client to access the Kubescape charts.
```bash
helm repo add kubescape https://kubescape.github.io/helm-charts
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install Kubescape Operator
steps.description:
Install the Kubescape operator using Helm. This command will create a namespace called 'kubescape' if it doesn't exist and install the operator in that namespace.
```bash
helm install kubescape-operator kubescape/kubescape-operator --namespace kubescape --create-namespace --version 1.30.5 --set capabilities.continuousScan=enable
```

**OpenShift clusters:** Kubescape pods require Security Context Constraints (SCCs). After install, grant appropriate SCCs:
```bash
# node-agent needs privileged SCC (hostPID, hostPath access)
kubectl create clusterrolebinding kubescape-scc-node-agent --clusterrole=system:openshift:scc:privileged --serviceaccount=kubescape:node-agent

# Other components run as UID 65532 (nonroot)
for sa in kubescape kubevuln operator storage; do
  kubectl create clusterrolebinding "kubescape-scc-${sa}" --clusterrole=system:openshift:scc:nonroot-v2 --serviceaccount="kubescape:${sa}"
done

# Restart all deployments and daemonset
kubectl -n kubescape rollout restart deployment kubescape kubevuln operator storage
kubectl -n kubescape rollout restart daemonset node-agent
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check that the Kubescape pods are running correctly in the 'kubescape' namespace.
```bash
kubectl get pods -n kubescape
```

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
Uninstall the Kubescape operator Helm release from the cluster.
```bash
helm uninstall kubescape-operator --namespace kubescape
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and resources
uninstall.description:
Delete any Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) that were created during the installation.
```bash
kubectl delete crd kubescape.*
```

uninstall.title: Remove the namespace
uninstall.description:
Delete the 'kubescape' namespace to clean up any remaining resources.
```bash
kubectl delete namespace kubescape
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, back up the current configuration and resources of the Kubescape operator.
```bash
kubectl get all -n kubescape -o yaml > kubescape-backup.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update the Helm repository
upgrade.description:
Update the Helm repository to ensure you have the latest charts.
```bash
helm repo update
```

upgrade.title: Upgrade the Kubescape operator
upgrade.description:
Upgrade the Kubescape operator to the latest version available in the Helm repository.
```bash
helm upgrade kubescape-operator kubescape/kubescape-operator --namespace kubescape --version 1.30.5
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
Check that the Kubescape pods are running the new version correctly.
```bash
kubectl get pods -n kubescape
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in Pending
troubleshooting.description:
If the Kubescape pods are stuck in 'Pending', check for insufficient resources in your cluster.
```bash
kubectl describe pod <pod-name> -n kubescape
```

troubleshooting.title: Kubescape operator crashes
troubleshooting.description:
If the Kubescape operator pod is crashing, check the logs for errors.
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> -n kubescape
```

troubleshooting.title: Helm installation fails
troubleshooting.description:
If the Helm installation fails, ensure that the Helm repository is correctly added and updated.
```bash
helm repo list
```

troubleshooting.title: Resource limits not applied
troubleshooting.description:
If resource limits are not applied, ensure that you edited the correct deployment and that the syntax is correct.
```bash
kubectl get deployment kubescape-operator -n kubescape -o yaml
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in Pending on OpenShift
troubleshooting.description:
On OpenShift clusters, Kubescape pods may fail to start due to Security Context Constraints (SCCs). Check events for SCC errors:
```bash
kubectl get events -n kubescape --field-selector reason=FailedCreate
```
Fix by granting appropriate SCCs — see the OpenShift note in the install step.

resolution.summary: A successful installation of Kubescape will have the operator running in the 'kubescape' namespace with all pods in a 'Running' state. You can verify this by checking the pod status with `kubectl get pods -n kubescape`.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add kubescape https://kubescape.github.io/helm-charts
helm repo update
helm install kubescape-operator kubescape/kubescape-operator --namespace kubescape --create-namespace --version 1.30.5 --set capabilities.continuousScan=enable

kubectl edit deployment kubescape-operator -n kubescape
# Add the following under spec.containers:
# resources:
#   limits:
#     memory: "512Mi"
#     cpu: "500m"

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running and Helm installed on your local machine.
